title: Injecting the Timestep into the Network
concept: Since denoising behavior differs at each noise level, the current timestep is encoded (similar to positional encoding) and fed into every U-Net layer.

exercises.prompt: When a doctor treats you, do they act the same way whether you have a tiny cough or a serious illness?

options.text: No, they adjust their treatment based on how bad it is
Yes, they always do the exact same thing
Doctors don't consider severity at all

exercises.explain: Just like a doctor adjusts treatment based on severity, a denoising network needs to adjust its behavior based on how much noise is present!

cards.title: Same Network, Different Jobs
cards.body: Imagine one worker who has to clean a room that's SUPER messy on Monday, and only slightly messy on Friday. Should they use the same cleaning strategy both days? A diffusion model's U-Net faces this exact problem — it must denoise images at wildly different noise levels, so it needs to know WHICH day (timestep) it's on!

cards.title: Timestep Injection
cards.body: Timestep injection means telling the U-Net exactly how noisy the current image is by encoding the timestep number (like t=1 or t=900) into a special vector, then feeding that vector into every layer of the network. This lets one single network behave differently depending on the noise level.

cards.title: A Volume Knob for Noise
cards.body: Think of the timestep encoding like a dial on a machine that tells every part of the machine 'how loud the noise is right now.' Every single room (layer) in the factory gets a peek at this dial's reading, so every worker adjusts their actions to match — gentle touch-ups near the end, big changes at the start.

cards.title: Positional Encoding, Reused
cards.body: Just like Transformers encode word POSITION in a sentence using sine and cosine waves, diffusion models encode the TIMESTEP number using a similar sinusoidal formula. This turns a simple number like '500' into a rich vector that the network can easily use to adjust its behavior at every layer.

cards.title: Myth: The Network Just 'Knows' the Noise Level
cards.body: Myth: The U-Net can automatically sense how noisy an image is just by looking at the pixels. Truth: The network needs to be explicitly TOLD the timestep through an encoded vector — without this injection, the same network would treat a barely-noisy image the same as a fully-noisy one, which leads to poor denoising!

cards.title: With vs. Without Timestep Info
cards.body: Feeding the timestep into every layer lets the network adapt its behavior smartly across the whole denoising journey, instead of guessing blindly.

left.text: Network treats every noise level the same — clumsy, one-size-fits-all denoising

right.text: Network knows exactly how noisy things are and adjusts each layer's behavior accordingly

exercises.prompt: Why does the U-Net need the timestep to be injected into its layers?

options.text: Because denoising behavior should differ depending on the noise level
Because it makes the images bigger
Because it removes the need for training
Because it speeds up image saving

exercises.explain: The network must act differently at high noise vs low noise, so it needs to know the timestep to adjust its behavior.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The timestep is only given to the very first layer of the U-Net, not the rest.
exercises.explain: False! The timestep encoding is fed into EVERY layer of the U-Net, not just the first one, so the whole network stays aware of the noise level.

exercises.sentence: The timestep is encoded using a method similar to ___ encoding, then injected into every U-Net layer.
exercises.explain: Timestep encoding borrows the same sinusoidal idea used in positional encoding from Transformers, turning a number into a useful vector.

exercises.prompt: What would likely happen if a diffusion model's U-Net was NOT given any timestep information?

options.text: It would denoise every noise level with the same fixed behavior, leading to poor results
It would automatically become faster to train
It would perfectly denoise every image regardless of noise level

exercises.explain: Without timestep info, the network can't tell how noisy the image currently is, so it can't adjust its denoising strategy appropriately.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'Diffusion models use one single U-Net for every timestep, but the network still behaves differently depending on the noise level because...' — how would you complete this sentence?

options.text: ...the timestep is encoded into a vector and injected into every layer, letting the network adapt its behavior
...each timestep secretly uses a totally different U-Net trained separately
...the network randomly guesses the noise level using trial and error
...the image resolution changes automatically at each timestep

exercises.explain: The key trick is timestep injection: encoding the timestep (like positional encoding) and feeding it into every layer lets one shared network flexibly adapt its denoising behavior to any noise level.
